title: HVAC Repair vs Replace Decision Guide
intended_use: Help homeowners and professionals make informed decisions about repairing or replacing HVAC equipment based on age, cost, efficiency, and practical factors.

the_50_percent_rule.rule: If the repair cost exceeds 50% of the replacement cost, replacement is usually the better investment.
nuance: For equipment over 15 years old, lower threshold to 30-40%
For equipment under 8 years old, repairs up to 60-70% may still make sense
Factor in expected remaining lifespan after repair

the_5000_rule.rule: Multiply the age of the equipment by the repair cost. If the result exceeds $5,000, consider replacing.
the_5000_rule.example: 12-year-old AC needs $500 repair: 12 × 500 = $6,000 → Lean toward replacement
the_5000_rule.note: This is a rough guideline, not absolute. Consider other factors below.

key_questions_to_ask: What's the repair history? (First issue vs recurring problems)
Is this a major component or minor part?
What's the efficiency rating vs modern equipment?
Are there comfort or air quality issues beyond this repair?
What are your plans for the home? (Selling soon vs staying long-term)

end_of_life_signs: Frequent repairs (more than once per season)
Efficiency noticeably declining (longer run times, higher bills)

heat_pumps.note: Shorter than AC-only because they run year-round

gas_furnaces.critical_end_of_life: Heat exchanger crack = immediate replacement required (CO risk)
end_of_life_signs: Flame sensor and igniter issues recurring

ductless_mini_splits.note: Indoor heads may need replacement before outdoor unit

usually_worth_repairing.reasoning: Cheap, common failure, doesn't indicate systemic issues

usually_worth_repairing.watch_for: Pitting means it's been arcing - normal end of life

usually_worth_repairing.reasoning: Common maintenance item, often just needs cleaning
usually_worth_repairing.note: If cleaning doesn't fix it, sensor replacement is still cheap

usually_worth_repairing.conditions: Only if system is under 10 years AND leak is found and fixed
usually_worth_repairing.warning: Repeated recharges = throwing money away. Find and fix the leak.

usually_worth_repairing.reasoning: Worth it if system is under 12-15 years old
usually_worth_repairing.note: ECM motors cost more ($600-$1,200) but last longer

usually_worth_repairing.reasoning: Common wear item on furnaces, easy fix
Solenoid is cheap; full valve replacement is different story
Worth it if heat pump is under 12 years old

repair_if: Rest of system is in good condition
case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.note: Compressor replacement often costs 50-70% of new system

replace_if: Coil has leaked refrigerant multiple times
case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.note: Consider replacing both indoor coil and outdoor unit together for proper matching

case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.reasoning: Similar decision tree to evaporator coil
case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.note: Coil-only replacement can cause mismatched system issues

case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.evaluate: Worth it if air handler is under 12 years old

replace_system_if: Secondary heat exchanger also showing wear
repair_if: Under warranty (many have 20-year or lifetime heat exchanger warranty)
case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.warning: Some contractors oversell heat exchanger cracks. Get second opinion.

case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.reasoning: Labor intensive. Evaluate system age carefully.
case_by_case_evaluate_carefully.usually_replace_system_if: Heat pump is over 12 years old

usually_replace_dont_repair.reasoning: R-22 is phased out. Even if compressor is replaced, refrigerant costs $75-$150/lb and rising
usually_replace_dont_repair.recommendation: Replace entire system with R-410A or newer R-454B

usually_replace_dont_repair.reasoning: Indicates systemic issues (contamination, restriction, electrical problems)
usually_replace_dont_repair.recommendation: Full system replacement to avoid third failure

usually_replace_dont_repair.reasoning: CO safety risk. Heat exchanger cost approaches new furnace cost
usually_replace_dont_repair.recommendation: Replace furnace. Modern furnaces are 95%+ efficient vs 80% on older units

usually_replace_dont_repair.reasoning: Multiple component failures indicate end of life
usually_replace_dont_repair.recommendation: System replacement more cost-effective than chasing problems

usually_replace_dont_repair.reasoning: Some manufacturers had widespread heat exchanger issues
usually_replace_dont_repair.recommendation: Check for active recalls. Replacement may be covered.

usually_replace_dont_repair.reasoning: Coastal corrosion damage is progressive and irreversible
usually_replace_dont_repair.recommendation: Replace with coated coils or consider mini-split for salt air environments

seer_comparison.description: Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio - higher is better

savings_example.note: Actual savings depend on climate, usage, and home efficiency

afue_comparison.description: Annual Fuel Utilization Efficiency - furnace efficiency

savings_example.note: Condensing furnaces require different venting (PVC vs metal flue)

payback_period_factors: Local utility rates (higher rates = faster payback)

rebates_and_incentives.federal: Check current tax credits at energystar.gov
rebates_and_incentives.utility: Many utilities offer $200-$1,500 rebates for high-efficiency equipment
rebates_and_incentives.state: Some states have additional incentive programs
rebates_and_incentives.tip: Ask contractor about available rebates BEFORE purchase

r22_phase_out_special_section.background: R-22 (Freon) production banned as of January 1, 2020. Only recycled/reclaimed R-22 available.

minor_leak_small_recharge.scenario: System needs 1-2 lbs R-22, leak can be repaired
minor_leak_small_recharge.recommendation: May be worth repairing if system is in otherwise good condition

major_leak_or_recurring.scenario: Large leak, compressor issue, or second/third recharge
major_leak_or_recurring.recommendation: Replace system. R-22 costs will continue rising.
major_leak_or_recurring.note: Every dollar spent on R-22 is money that won't go toward new equipment

compressor_failure.reasoning: New compressor + R-22 recharge could exceed $3,000-$4,000

retrofit_not_recommended.explanation: Some suggest 'drop-in' refrigerant replacements (R-407C, R-422D, etc.)
problems: Some are being phased out too
Requires oil change and system flush
retrofit_not_recommended.better_option: Put that money toward a new R-410A or R-454B system

guidance: New HVAC can add value and appeal to buyers
However, you won't recoup 100% of replacement cost
If current system works: consider repair and disclose age to buyers
If current system is problematic: replacement prevents deal-killing inspection issues
selling_home_soon.tip: Get system serviced and documented before listing

guidance: Invest in quality replacement when needed
Higher efficiency pays back over time
Proper sizing via Manual J is crucial
Factor in tenant comfort and retention
Document all repairs for tax purposes
Perfect time to replace aging equipment
New load calculation (Manual J) required for additions
May need larger system or second zone
home_renovation.procalcs_note: Contact ProCalcs for Manual J load calculations: 772-882-5700

proper_sizing.critical_warning: NEVER accept 'rule of thumb' sizing (400-600 sq ft per ton)
proper_sizing.requirement: Insist on Manual J load calculation
oversizing_problems: Higher upfront cost for no benefit
undersizing_problems: Can't keep up on extreme days
proper_sizing.procalcs_service: ProCalcs provides professional Manual J calculations: 772-882-5700 / tom@procalcs.net

matching_components.warning: Mixing old and new components reduces efficiency and can void warranties
matching_components.best_practice: Replace as matched system (outdoor unit + indoor coil at minimum)

when_to_address: Visible damage, disconnections, or crushed sections
ductwork_evaluation.note: New high-efficiency equipment won't perform well with poor ductwork

contractor_selection.get_multiple_quotes: At least 3 quotes from licensed contractors
red_flags: Quote without seeing the home or doing calculations

cost_ranges_2024_2025.disclaimer: Costs vary significantly by region, brand, and complexity. These are rough national averages.

central_air_conditioner.includes: Outdoor unit, indoor coil, installation, permits

heat_pump_system.note: Higher cost offset by heating AND cooling capability

ductless_mini_split.note: Great for additions, rooms without ductwork, or zoned comfort

step_1.question: Is the equipment over 15 years old?
step_1.if_yes: Lean toward replacement unless repair is very minor (<$300)

step_2.question: Is it an R-22 refrigerant system?

step_3.question: Is the repair cost more than 50% of replacement cost?

step_4.question: Is this a safety issue (heat exchanger crack, CO detected, electrical hazard)?

step_5.question: Has this equipment needed repairs more than once in the past 2 years?
step_5.if_yes: Consider replacement - pattern of failures indicates declining health

step_6.question: Is this a major component (compressor, heat exchanger, coil)?
step_6.if_yes: Carefully evaluate with age and other factors
step_6.if_no: Repair is likely the right choice

when_contractors_recommend_replacement: Multiple component failures indicating system decline
Compressor failure on older or R-22 system
Customer complaining about efficiency/comfort even after repairs
when_contractors_recommend_repair: Simple component failures on newer equipment
Customer has budget constraints (ethical contractors will repair what they can)

questions_to_ask_contractor: What's the expected lifespan of this equipment after repair?
What would a new system cost for comparison?
Is my refrigerant type still available/affordable?
Are there any rebates available if I replace?
Can I get this in writing?

second_opinion_triggers: Contractor immediately pushes replacement on first visit
Recommendation to 'add refrigerant' without finding leak
Something feels off about the diagnosis

summary_rules_of_thumb: Under 8 years old: Almost always repair (unless major safety issue or R-22)
8-15 years old: Evaluate carefully using the factors above
Over 15 years old: Lean toward replacement for major repairs
R-22 system: Replace unless repair is truly minor
Heat exchanger crack: Replace the furnace (safety first)
Multiple repairs same season: System is telling you it's done
Simple parts (capacitor, contactor, igniter): Almost always repair
Major parts (compressor, coils): Do the math carefully
When in doubt: Get a second opinion from a different contractor

ductwork_and_distribution_impact.why_it_matters: A new high-efficiency system on bad ducts performs like a sports car on flat tires.
key_checks: Undersized returns causing high static pressure
Comfort issues tied to distribution (hot/cold rooms)
Flex duct kinks or crushed sections
lean_toward_replacement_or_redesign_when: Ducts are damaged, contaminated, or inaccessible for repair
Return pathways are missing (doors slam, whistling, stuffy rooms)
repair_ok_when: Duct system is confirmed sound and sized appropriately
Comfort issues are isolated and easily corrected
ductwork_and_distribution_impact.key_message: If we replace equipment without fixing airflow, you'll still have comfort problems and the new system will be stressed.

envelope_and_load_changes.why_it_matters: If the house changed, the load changed. That can turn a 'replace like-for-like' into a comfort problem.
triggers_requiring_new_load_calculation: Major shade tree removal or new sun exposure
envelope_and_load_changes.why_it_matters_for_sizing: Sizing based on old conditions can lead to oversizing (short cycling, humidity issues) or undersizing (comfort complaints).
envelope_and_load_changes.procalcs_note: Contact ProCalcs for updated Manual J calculations after home improvements: 772-882-5700

humidity_and_iaq_as_decision_drivers.why_it_matters: Sometimes the decision isn't 'broken vs not' but 'healthy vs not healthy.'
signs_replacement_makes_more_sense: Persistent high indoor humidity despite correct sizing attempts
Recurring mold/mildew smells from air handler or ducts
Severe dust or IAQ complaints tied to return leakage or poor filtration design
upgrade_considerations: Variable-speed air handler for longer runtimes and better dehumidification
Media filter cabinet (4-5 inch) instead of restrictive 1-inch high-MERV
Balanced ventilation (ERV/HRV) in tighter, energy-efficient homes

electrical_capacity_considerations.why_it_matters: Electrification and heat pumps often fail because the electrical side was ignored.
key_checks: Main panel capacity and available breaker spaces
replacement_plan_must_include: Permitting timeline for electrical upgrades if needed
electrical_capacity_considerations.red_flag: If a contractor quotes a heat pump swap with no mention of electrical capacity, the quote is incomplete.

reliability_patterns_vs_isolated_failure.why_it_matters: Pros don't just look at one broken part. They look for patterns.
systemic_failure_indicators: Multiple major parts in 18 months (capacitor + contactor + compressor symptoms)
Recurring refrigerant loss without permanent leak repair
Repeated high head pressure trips after coil cleanings
High static pressure causing repeated blower failures
History of water damage to control boards from drain issues
isolated_failure_indicators: Single component failure with no history (contactor/capacitor at normal lifespan)
Wear items at expected life stage
Clear root cause that can be permanently fixed (debris, blocked drain)

decision_impact.systemic_pattern: Lean toward replacement - chasing individual repairs won't solve underlying issues
decision_impact.isolated_failure: Repair is usually the right choice

replace_bias_triggers: Oversized systems causing chronic humidity complaints
Air handler/duct microbial growth from wet operation
upgrade_priorities: Ducts inside conditioned space or sealed/encapsulated attic

replace_bias_triggers: Condenser chronic overheating due to placement/airflow limits
Dust-related coil damage and repeated compressor stress
upgrade_priorities: High EER focus for peak conditions
UV protection for exposed insulation and linesets

replace_bias_triggers: Heat pump can't carry load and relies heavily on backup heat
Frozen/icing problems due to poor outdoor placement
upgrade_priorities: Cold-climate heat pumps with verified low-temp capacity
Dual-fuel where gas is cost-effective and winters are severe
Outdoor unit elevation and snow/defrost drainage planning

replace_bias_triggers: No cooling capacity for modern heat events
Damp-season mold/mustiness due to low runtime
upgrade_priorities: Heat pump retrofits (often best fit for climate)
Ventilation and filtration planning (wildfire smoke events)

replace_bias_triggers: Outdoor coil repeatedly leaking from corrosion
Combustion appliances with chronic drafting/venting issues
Heat pump performance issues due to extreme temperature swings
upgrade_priorities: Snow drift and roof dump avoidance for outdoor units

common_gotchas: Warranty may require registration within 60-90 days of install
Some warranties require licensed contractor maintenance records
Compressor/coils may be covered but labor is not
Mismatched indoor/outdoor components can reduce warranty coverage
'Parts warranty' doesn't cover duct defects or installation problems
homeowner_questions_to_ask: Is labor covered? For how long?
Is the warranty transferable if I sell the home?
What maintenance documentation do I need to keep?
Is this a matched AHRI-rated system?

how_pros_estimate: Compressor sound and amp draw trends
History of refrigerant additions (proxy for ongoing leak risk)
Static pressure and airflow health (stress indicator)
practical_outputs: 'Likely stable for 1-3 years with minor repairs'
'High risk of major failure within 12 months'
'Replace now is economically safer than repeated repairs'
remaining_useful_life_assessment.rule_of_thumb: If the system has multiple stress indicators, treat remaining life as short even if it 'still runs.'

common_options: Manufacturer or contractor financing (promotional 0% APR periods)
HELOC or home improvement loans (often better rates, longer terms)
Utility rebates and efficiency incentives (require specific models/documentation)
Federal tax credits (heat pumps/efficiency upgrades - verify current rules at energystar.gov)
practical_guidance: Ask for the total installed price, not just 'monthly payment'
Compare same scope: duct fixes, electrical upgrades, permits, thermostat, filtration
Be wary of financing that hides inflated equipment pricing

red_flags: 'You need a whole new system' without showing measurements or evidence
Pressure to decide immediately ('today only' pricing)
Blaming refrigerant type alone without diagnosing leaks or airflow
Quoting SEER upgrades without addressing duct/static pressure limitations
Selling UV lights and IAQ gadgets as a cure for humidity problems
Offering to 'top off refrigerant' repeatedly without leak repair plan
Claiming heat exchanger is cracked without showing proof and CO test results
green_flags: Documents findings (static pressure, temp split, combustion test, amperage)
Explains options with pros/cons and expected outcomes
Talks about airflow and duct health, not just equipment
Provides model numbers and AHRI match-ups
Gives clear scope and permit plan

best_times_to_replace: Shoulder seasons (spring and fall) when demand is lower
Before peak season to avoid emergency pricing and limited inventory
why_timing_matters: Time to correct duct and electrical issues properly

goals: Restore safety and basic comfort quickly
recommended_actions: Ask for a 'stabilize now' option plus a 'replace later' plan
Request temporary solutions if replacement lead time is long
Get at least one second opinion if time allows

goals: Optimize comfort, efficiency, and long-term cost
recommended_actions: Schedule Manual J and duct review early
Plan improvements: media filtration, dehumidification, or ventilation if needed

scope_and_design: Is this a matched AHRI-rated system? What's the AHRI certificate number?
What's included: permits, line set, pad, electrical disconnect, float switch, thermostat?
Are you evaluating duct leakage and static pressure? Will you measure and document it?
Will you verify airflow at startup (CFM/static) and provide the readings?
quality_and_protection: How will you protect the home during install (dust, flooring, debris)?
What's your condensate overflow protection plan (especially in attics)?
Will you add surge protection if the area has outages or lightning?
warranty_and_service: What's the parts warranty and what's the labor warranty?
Is the warranty transferable if I sell?
Do you register the equipment and provide proof?
Do you offer a maintenance plan and what does it actually include?
pricing_clarity: Can you itemize: equipment, labor, duct modifications, electrical upgrades, permit fees?
What change orders could occur and why?
What's the total installed price (not just monthly payment)?

what_experienced_techs_look_for: Coil condition and leak likelihood (especially microchannel and coastal corrosion)
Compressor sound and current draw trends
Heat exchanger integrity and combustion safety (furnaces)
Water damage history to control boards and drain pans
Installation quality issues (incorrect airflow, poor drainage, undersized returns)

systemic_indicators: Repeated failures tied to airflow (high static pressure, dirty blower wheel)
Recurring condensate overflow causing control board damage
Chronic high head pressure despite clean coil
Refrigerant loss over time (leak not properly resolved)
isolated_indicators: Single known-failure part at normal life stage
Clear one-time event damage (surge) with no recurring pattern

remaining_useful_life_inputs: Age and environment severity (coastal, desert, extreme cold)
Runtime intensity (long cooling seasons vs mild climates)
Maintenance history (coil cleanliness, filters, drain management)
Major component health signals (amps, noise, vibration, heat)
Probability-weighted failure risk (what's likely to fail next and cost)
